nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal freshwater streams in rocky areas with abundant aquatic vegetation; commonly found near river mouths, hiding among pebbles, rocks, and crevices. Larvae are reported to develop in brackish or marine waters before juveniles migrate back upstream (amphidromous life cycle typical of Varunidae).

decorPreferences: sand or fine gravel substrate for burrowing
rockwork, pebbles, and caves for hiding and molting shelter
live plants tolerated (will not cut foliage but may uproot fine-rooted species)
driftwood and leaf litter for biofilm grazing
tightly-fitting lid with covered intakes (climbing/escape risk)

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivorous scavenger that readily accepts sinking pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables, frozen or freeze-dried meaty foods, and biofilm grazed from rockwork. The dense bristle tufts on the claws — the same tufts that give the species its common name — are waved rhythmically through the water column and are commonly described as filter-feeding apparatus, though sources differ on whether the species is a true obligate filter-feeder; in captivity they are generally fed as scavengers. Calcium supplementation through cuttlebone or mineral-rich foods supports successful moulting.

goodWith: small peaceful nano fish (chili rasboras, ember tetras, pygmy rasboras)
adult Amano and Neocaridina shrimp (with caution)
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that may prey on the crab during a moult
very small dwarf shrimp and shrimplets (occasional opportunistic predation reported)

summary: The Freshwater Pom Pom Crab (Ptychognathus barbatus) is a small, fully aquatic Asian crab reaching about 2.5 cm, named for the dense bristle tufts on its claws. Peaceful and well suited to planted nano community tanks, it is unrelated to the marine boxer crab (Lybia tessellata), which shares the common name.
careNotes: The Freshwater Pom Pom Crab is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly true freshwater crabs because, unlike most 'freshwater' crabs sold in the hobby, it is fully aquatic and does not require a land area. The species is widely distributed across coastal streams from Madagascar through Southeast Asia to New Caledonia, and is regularly available through specialty importers. The common name 'Pom Pom Crab' is also used for the unrelated marine boxer crab Lybia tessellata, which has entirely different care requirements and lives in saltwater; the scientific name Ptychognathus barbatus is the reliable way to distinguish between the two in trade listings. Freshwater Pom Pom Crabs are competent climbers and will exploit any gap above the waterline, so a tightly-fitting lid and covered filter intakes are strongly recommended. Like all invertebrates, they are highly sensitive to copper; copper-based medications and plant fertilisers with elevated copper levels should be avoided in any tank housing them. Sand or fine gravel of at least a few centimetres depth supports their burrowing behaviour.
breedingNotes: Generally considered impractical in standard home aquaria. As with most Varunidae, the species is believed to be amphidromous: females may carry eggs in freshwater, but the hatched larvae require brackish or marine salinity to develop through several planktonic zoeal stages before metamorphosing into juveniles and migrating back to freshwater. No reliable captive-breeding protocol is documented in the trade. Lost limbs are reported to regenerate fully over 1–2 moults.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary did not have a dedicated species page for Ptychognathus barbatus and the site root returned HTTP 403. Substituted with Aquatic Arts as primary cross-check secondary.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Fallback secondary used in place of Shrimp Farm. Confirmed adult size ~1 inch (2.5 cm), fully aquatic status, tank-size guidance (5 gal min), peaceful temperament, escape risk, omnivorous scavenger diet, and pH 6.8–7.2 / temperature 20–25°C.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed family Varunidae, native range across Asia/Madagascar/New Caledonia, carapace 15–25 mm, lifespan up to ~1 year, water parameters (22–28°C, pH 6.8–7.2, GH 6–8, KH 2–6), and amphidromous brackish-larval breeding.

additional.name: Shrimpy Business
additional.notes: Fourth cross-check source. Confirmed adult size up to 1 inch, peaceful temperament, fully aquatic with climbing/escape ability, and reported a minority view that the species may be obligately brackish.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults are reported to moult roughly every 3–6 weeks; juveniles moult more frequently. Lost limbs are reported to regenerate fully within one to two moults. Shed exoskeletons should be left in the tank as a calcium source. Sudden parameter swings (especially in GH or salinity) are commonly associated with failed moults.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Amphidromous life cycle is suspected: larvae are reported to require brackish or marine water (~SG 1.015–1.020) to develop, then would need to be acclimated back to freshwater as post-larvae. Without a dedicated brackish rearing setup, reproduction is effectively impossible in a standard freshwater tank, and no reliable captive-breeding protocol is documented.
